subject: Math

lesson.story_mission: Help the Measurement Makers label classroom objects with accurate lengths.
lesson.mini_lesson: Measure length by lining up one end at zero, reading the endpoint, and naming the unit. Estimate when an exact ruler is not needed.
lesson.objective: Measure and estimate lengths using inches and centimeters, then compare length differences.
lesson.concept_explanation: A length is the distance from one end of an object to the other. The unit tells what size marks you counted.
lesson.common_misconception: Learners may start at the edge of the ruler instead of zero or mix inches and centimeters.
lesson.real_world_connection: Measuring helps choose materials for crafts, classroom objects, and building projects.
lesson.lesson_summary: Start at zero, count same-size units, record the unit, and subtract to compare lengths.
hints: Line up the object at 0.
Use the unit named in the question.
For differences, subtract the shorter length from the longer length.

worked_examples.text: A pencil from 0 to 5 inches is 5 inches long.
A string from 3 cm to 10 cm is 7 cm long because 10 - 3 = 7.
If one ribbon is 9 inches and another is 5 inches, the difference is 4 inches.

hint_ladder: Use the unit named in the question.
For differences, subtract the shorter length from the longer length.

misconception_bank.starts_not_at_zero: Learner may count from the ruler edge or first mark instead of zero.
misconception_bank.unit_confusion: Learner may give inches when the ruler is centimeters, or centimeters when it is inches.
misconception_bank.estimate_error: Learner may choose an unreasonable length for a familiar object.
misconception_bank.length_difference_confusion: Learner may report one object length instead of subtracting to find the difference.

game_theme.title: Measure Length
game_theme.mission_intro: Help the Measurement Makers label classroom objects with accurate lengths.

guided_practice.prompt: A pencil starts at 0 and ends at 5 inches. How long is the pencil?

Use the unit named in the question.
For differences, subtract the shorter length from the longer length.

hints: Line up the object at 0.
Use the unit named in the question.
For differences, subtract the shorter length from the longer length.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: A reasonable estimate is 36 inches.

question_audio.text: A classroom door is about how many inches wide?
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.prompt: A red ribbon is 9 inches. A blue ribbon is 5 inches. How many inches longer is the red ribbon?
hints: Line up the object at 0.
Use the unit named in the question.
For differences, subtract the shorter length from the longer length.

feedback.incorrect: Use the visual model and try again.

questions.explanation: Find the difference by subtracting the shorter length from the longer length: 4 inches.

story.text: Listen to this page. You are starting a math mission about Measure Length. Look at the story and think about what the characters need to solve. Math missions are easier when you name the numbers and the model first. Press Start Mission when you are ready.

lesson.text: This lesson teaches Measure Length. Look at the ruler and connect each part to the math words. Use the vocabulary on the page to explain your thinking. Remember to check place value, units, or symbols before you answer. Press Next to continue.

watch.text: This page lets you watch a worked example for Measure Length. Follow the model one step at a time. Say what each number means before you look at the final answer. If a symbol appears, read it in words like plus, minus, equals, greater than, or less than. Press Next to continue.
watch.label: Read This Page

demo.text: This demo helps you try Measure Length with support. Build or study the model first, then connect it to the question. A good strategy is to explain the answer in math words before choosing. Press Next when you are ready for practice.
